research.summary: EV adoption in the US is sharply uneven by state. In Q1 2025, EVs were 9.6% of new light-duty vehicle sales nationally, but California alone hit 23.7% — five-plus times the share in laggard states like West Virginia and North Dakota. California also accounts for roughly 35% of all light-duty BEVs on US roads (1.26 million of 3.56 million as of end-2023, per DOE AFDC/Experian). West Coast and Northeast states (CA, WA, OR, CO, NJ, MA, VT) lead on both share and per-capita; Plains/Appalachian states (ND, WV, MS, WY) lag. The two best open datasets are DOE AFDC's annual "Electric Vehicle Registrations by State" (Experian-derived, BEVs only) and the Alliance for Automotive Innovation's quarterly "Get Connected" report (new-vehicle market share, BEV+PHEV).

stories.headline: One in three US BEVs lives in California
stories.body: California ended 2023 with 1.26 million battery-electric cars — about 35% of the entire US BEV stock, per DOE AFDC. In Q1 2025, nearly one in four new cars sold in California was a plug-in (23.7% EV share, the highest of any state). The Advanced Clean Cars II rule, HOV-lane access, generous state rebates, and the densest public-charging network in the country all stack the deck.
stories.source_name: DOE AFDC + Alliance for Automotive Innovation Q1 2025

stories.headline: The Pacific Northwest's quiet EV powerhouse
stories.body: Washington hit 19.7% EV share of new sales in Q1 2025 — second only to California. With 152,000 BEVs on the road by end-2023 and 1,805 BEVs per 100,000 residents, the state pairs cheap hydropower with a state sales-tax exemption for EVs under a price cap. Seattle, Bellevue, and the I-5 corridor concentrate most of the fleet.
stories.source_name: Alliance for Automotive Innovation Q1 2025; DOE AFDC

stories.headline: Colorado leapfrogs the coasts on incentives
stories.body: Colorado's EV share of new-vehicle sales hit 19.2% in Q1 2025, third nationally. The state stacks a $5,000 state tax credit on top of the federal $7,500, plus an extra credit for vehicles under $35,000. BEV stock roughly doubled between 2021 and 2023 to 90,000 vehicles.
stories.source_name: Alliance for Automotive Innovation Q1 2025; DOE AFDC

stories.headline: Big stock, middling share
stories.body: Florida has the second-largest BEV fleet in the country (255,000 cars in 2023) thanks to sheer population and Tesla's strong retail presence, but EV share of NEW sales was only 10.1% in Q1 2025 — barely above the national average. Florida has no state EV purchase incentive and limited utility programs.
stories.source_name: DOE AFDC; Alliance for Automotive Innovation Q1 2025

stories.headline: Texas: huge fleet, slow share growth
stories.body: Texas had 230,000 BEVs registered at end-2023 — third nationally — buoyed by Tesla's Austin Gigafactory and a strong Model Y owner base. But the state imposes a $200 annual EV registration surcharge, has no purchase incentive, and EV share of new sales lags the national average outside Austin.

stories.headline: The laggards: under 3,000 EVs each
stories.body: West Virginia had just 2,758 BEVs registered at end-2023; North Dakota had 959 — together less than 0.4% of California's fleet. Cold winters cut range, public charging is sparse, pickups dominate the new-vehicle mix, and neither state offers a purchase incentive. Both also impose annual EV registration fees ($200 in WV, $120 in ND).

stories.headline: Small state, outsized share
stories.body: Vermont registered fewer than 8,000 BEVs in total (2023) but hit 10.9% EV share of new sales in Q1 2025 — well above the US average. The state's Drive Electric Vermont program, utility rebates from Green Mountain Power, and a CARB-aligned ZEV mandate punch above its weight.
stories.source_name: Alliance for Automotive Innovation Q1 2025; DOE AFDC

stories.headline: Tesla Model Y is the best-selling EV in nearly every state
stories.body: In 2024, the Model Y (~373,000 US sales) and Model 3 (~190,000) were the two top-selling EVs nationally. State-level VIN data from Atlas EV Hub shows the Model Y is the #1 plug-in in nearly every reporting state, with Ford F-150 Lightning and Mustang Mach-E, Hyundai Ioniq 5, and Chevy Equinox EV trading places for runner-up.
stories.source_name: Cox Automotive / Kelley Blue Book; Atlas EV Hub

caveats: AFDC's 'EV Registration Counts by State' covers BEVs ONLY — PHEVs are explicitly excluded per the worksheet footnote. Headline 'EV stock' numbers therefore understate plug-in totals by roughly 25-35% depending on state.

Alliance for Automotive Innovation 'Get Connected' percentages cover BEV+PHEV+FCEV combined ('EV share'); the press release does not always break out BEV vs PHEV by state. For BEV-only share, use S&P Global Mobility or Experian directly (paywalled).

AFDC stock data is end-of-2023 (released Sept 2024); 2024 update expected Sept 2025. Q1 2025 share data is the freshest signal available as of the research date (June 2026 — but published April 2025).

Per-capita BEV counts cited (CA 3,026 / WA 1,805 / HI 1,686 per 100k) come from a Visual Capitalist analysis of DOE+Census data, not a primary source. Re-derive from AFDC stock + Census ACS population for the production site.

Texas, New York, Georgia, West Virginia, and North Dakota are NOT individually listed in the Q1 2025 Get Connected press release (only the 11 states above 10% share are named). Full state percentages require the underlying PDF or dashboard.

Stock-based metrics (registrations on the road) and flow-based metrics (share of new sales) tell different stories — Florida and Texas rank high on stock but middling on new-sales share. Visualize both side by side.

Atlas EV Hub state-level model data depends on each state's DMV cooperation — coverage is incomplete (e.g. no California VIN-level data in the public Atlas dataset). For California model-level mix, use CA Energy Commission's ZEV Sales Dashboard instead.

audit.summary: All load-bearing numbers verified against primary sources. National Q1 2025 share (9.6%) and total (374,841) confirmed in the Alliance for Automotive Innovation Q1 2025 PDF. State Q1 2025 shares (CA 23.7, WA 19.7, CO 19.2, NJ 13.1) all match the same report. AFDC 2023 BEV stock figures (CA 1,256,646; WA 152,101; CO 90,083; NJ 134,753) confirmed and correctly labeled as BEV-only. Experian 9.2% for 2024 confirmed. Per-capita figures match Visual Capitalist's 2024 article. One minor flag: the EIA fuel_ev.pdf URL resolves to "Table F54: Electric light-duty vehicles overview, 2024" — title slightly differs from the "BEV stock and energy-equivalent consumption" description, but the dataset is real and the URL works.

research.summary: As of 2025, the U.S. has ~249,660 public Level 2 + DC Fast charging ports across ~80k stations (DOE AFDC). California dominates with ~64.7k ports (26% of national L2+DCFC), followed by New York (~20k), Florida (~15k), Texas (~12k), and Massachusetts (~11k). The $5B federal NEVI program was paused Feb 2025, restarted under revised guidance Aug 2025; only ~57 NEVI-funded stations (384 ports) across 15 states were open by early 2025. Eleven states plus DC have formally adopted California's Advanced Clean Cars II / ZEV mandate (100% ZEV new-car sales by 2035), though June 2025 Congressional Review Act resolutions revoked the underlying EPA waiver and litigation is ongoing. State purchase incentives have shifted toward income-targeted rebates: California's CVRP closed Nov 2023 (replaced by DCAP/CC4A), Colorado raised its VXC point-of-sale rebate to $9,000 (Nov 2025), and New Jersey's Charge Up NJ pays up to $4,000.

stories.headline: The original ZEV state, and still the gravity well of US charging
stories.body: California passed Advanced Clean Cars II in August 2022, requiring 35% ZEV new-car sales by 2026, 68% by 2030, and 100% by 2035. As of late 2025 the state hosts ~64,700 public L2+DCFC ports per AFDC, and the California Energy Commission counts over 201,000 public and shared ports across all levels statewide. The original CVRP rebate closed in November 2023; equity-focused replacements (DCAP, Clean Cars 4 All) continue. A June 2025 Congressional Review Act resolution revoked the EPA waiver underpinning ACC II, and California is suing to restore it.

stories.headline: First state outside California to adopt ACC II
stories.body: In December 2022 Vermont became the first state to adopt both Advanced Clean Cars II and Advanced Clean Trucks in a single rulemaking. With only ~647,000 residents, Vermont has built ~1,376 public L2+DCFC ports — roughly 213 ports per 100k people, one of the highest per-capita densities in the country.

stories.headline: Adopted ACC II in 2022 with state law backing 2030 ZEV goal
stories.body: Washington adopted ACC II in December 2022. State legislation also sets an aspirational goal that all new light-duty vehicles model year 2030 or later sold in the state be electric — five years ahead of the ACC II 2035 deadline. The state has ~8,600 public L2+DCFC ports.

stories.headline: Emergency rulemaking, second-largest charging network in US
stories.body: New York adopted ACC II by emergency rulemaking in 2022 and codified it via DEC in 2023. The state hosts ~20,100 public L2+DCFC ports — second only to California — and through Charge NY and NYSERDA Drive Clean offers up to $2,000 in point-of-sale rebates.

stories.headline: Adopted ACC II Nov 2023, $1,500-$4,000 Charge Up rebates
stories.body: New Jersey officially adopted ACC II on November 21, 2023. Charge Up NJ pays $1,500 on a new EV purchase or lease, plus up to $2,500 more for income-qualified buyers, and $250 toward a Level 2 charger. The state has ~6,000 public L2+DCFC ports.

stories.headline: ACC II adopted; nation's most generous state EV rebate stack
stories.body: Colorado adopted ACC II in 2023. Starting November 2025 the Vehicle Exchange Colorado (VXC) program pays $9,000 at point of sale for a new EV (up from $6,000) and $6,000 for a used EV; stacked with the Innovative Motor Vehicle Credit, eligible buyers can get up to $15,000 off a new EV using state programs alone.

stories.headline: Adopted ACC II; MOR-EV rebate up to $3,500
stories.body: Massachusetts adopted ACC II via DEP regulation in late 2023. The MOR-EV program pays $3,500 for a new EV under $55,000 with an extra $1,500 for income-qualified buyers. The state has ~11,000 public L2+DCFC ports — fifth-most in the US.

stories.headline: Adopted ACC II in 2022; Charge Ahead rebate paused
stories.body: Oregon DEQ adopted ACC II in December 2022. The Oregon Clean Vehicle Rebate (up to $7,500 for low/moderate income via Charge Ahead) was paused in May 2024 due to funding constraints, with status uncertain into 2025-26.

stories.headline: Adopted ACC II in 2023; excise tax credit up to $3,000
stories.body: Maryland adopted ACC II in 2023 via MDE regulation. The state's Clean Cars Act provides an excise tax credit of up to $3,000 on EVs under $50,000 MSRP, subject to annual funding caps.

stories.headline: Adopted ACC II in November 2023
stories.body: New Mexico's Environmental Improvement Board voted to adopt ACC II on November 16, 2023, requiring 43% ZEV sales by model year 2027 and 82% by model year 2032. New Mexico also offers a state EV tax credit of up to $3,000.

stories.headline: Adopted ACC II at end of 2023
stories.body: Delaware DNREC adopted ACC II in December 2023, becoming one of the smallest states to do so. The Clean Vehicle Rebate Program pays up to $2,500 for a new BEV.

stories.headline: Adopted ACC II; DRIVE EV rebate up to $1,500
stories.body: Rhode Island DEM finalized ACC II adoption in late 2023. The DRIVE EV program offers $1,500 for a new BEV plus $1,500 income-qualified adder.

stories.headline: Finalized ACC II adoption end of 2023
stories.body: DC finalized ACC II at the end of 2023 — the only non-state jurisdiction to formally adopt it. DC has ~1,100 public L2+DCFC ports across just 68 square miles, one of the densest networks in the country.

stories.headline: Adopted then exited: Youngkin pulled VA out at end of 2024
stories.body: Virginia adopted ACC II via 2021 legislation tying it to California's standards. In June 2024 Governor Glenn Youngkin announced Virginia would no longer participate in the program after the end of 2024 — the first state to formally exit. Litigation over the move is ongoing.

stories.headline: $5B federal charging program: paused, then restarted in 2026
stories.body: The IIJA's National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Formula Program provides $5 billion over five years to build DC fast-charging every 50 miles along designated Alternative Fuel Corridors. FHWA had apportioned $3.3 billion to states through FY2025, but the Trump administration paused the program in February 2025 pending review. Updated guidance issued August 2025 — granting states more flexibility on station spacing and corridor completion — unfroze funding, with the network effectively rebooting in 2026. Only ~57 stations (~384 ports) across 15 states had opened under NEVI by early 2025, with Ohio and New York leading.

caveats: AFDC port counts are from the DOE Station Locator state table fetched 2025; values shift daily as new stations come online and the table is refreshed.

AFDC counts only public + shared private ports it indexes; California's state-level count (~201,180 ports as of Sep 2025) is higher because CEC includes additional shared-use chargers not all in AFDC.

DC Fast port counts differ across sources: AFDC state table shows ~73,442 total ports (L2+DCFC sum here uses AFDC); industry trackers (Paren) reported ~60,000 DCFC stalls mid-2025 and >70,000 by year-end — definitional differences (stalls vs ports, public-only) drive the gap.

In June 2025 Congress passed Congressional Review Act resolutions revoking the EPA waivers underpinning ACC II; lawsuits are pending. States that adopted ACC II remain on the books but enforceability of ZEV mandates is now contested.
Federal EV tax credits (new and used) expired for vehicles acquired after September 30, 2025; state incentives are increasingly the only purchase subsidy.
Virginia adopted ACC II then exited at end of 2024 under Governor Youngkin — count VA as a former adopter, not current.
Ports-per-capita figures use AFDC ports divided by approximate state population; not authoritative — for a published site recompute against Census ACS 2024 estimates.
NEVI 'stations open' counts move quickly; the 57-station figure is as of February 10, 2025 per CRS — by late 2025 more stations had energized but a comprehensive count is not yet published.

audit.summary: AFDC port totals are close to live values but the snapshot date matters — AFDC updates daily, so the exact numbers drift. As of 2026-06-16, the live AFDC state table shows US total 250,390 (claimed 249,660), and California 65,018 (claimed 64,726). L2/DCFC US splits match exactly (176,218 / 73,442). State-level numbers for NY, FL, TX, MA, WA, GA, CO are all within ~100 ports of live values — fine if labeled as a snapshot. The CEC California 201,180 figure (Sept 2025) is confirmed verbatim. Two URL/dataset issues: (1) the FHWA NEVI URL is 404 — correct path is fhwa.dot.gov/environment/nevi/ (no "alternative_fuel_corridors"); (2) AFDC dataset 10972 is described as "quarterly" but the published series is annual (through 2023). The CARB 2019 PDF link returns 403 and is outdated — the live CARB dashboard page is the better citation. CleanCarStates.org and the AFDC station locator URLs verified live.

research.domain: Global country-level EV adoption (cars, BEV+PHEV unless noted)
research.summary: The authoritative open dataset for country-level EV adoption is the IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 (released May 2025, covering 2010-2024 history plus projections). Our World in Data republishes the IEA series as clean CSVs under CC BY 4.0 — the most practical machine-readable source for a Python pipeline. In 2024, electric cars (BEV+PHEV) reached 21% of new global car sales, led by Norway at ~92% (88% BEV + ~3% PHEV), with China at 48%, Europe in the 20-58% range, and the US lagging at ~10%. Global EV stock surpassed 56 million cars in 2024, with China holding ~32 million (over half).

datasets.name: Global EV Outlook 2025 - Data product
datasets.coverage: All countries with reported EV data + regions + World; 2010-2024 historical, projections to 2035; cars, vans, trucks, buses, 2/3-wheelers; BEV and PHEV powertrains
datasets.format: CSV (XLSX also available); requires free IEA account login to download

stories.headline: Nine in ten new cars are electric — and most are pure battery
stories.body: Norway is the world's clearest EV success story. In 2024 about 92% of new cars sold were plug-ins, with 88% pure battery-electric (BEV) and just under 3% plug-in hybrid. Decades of tax exemptions, free tolls/parking, bus-lane access and a fully built-out charging network made the EV the obvious default car. Norway is on track to be the first country where new internal-combustion car sales effectively end.

stories.headline: Nearly half of new cars sold are electric — and China holds most of the world's EVs
stories.body: China hit 48% EV sales share in 2024, up from under 6% in 2020. Its fleet of ~32 million electric cars is more than half of the global total of ~56 million. BYD, SAIC, Geely and others now export aggressively, and PHEVs/EREVs are the fastest-growing segment.
stories.source_name: IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 via OWID

stories.headline: The laggard among rich markets — about 10% of new cars are electric
stories.body: US EV share crept from 7.7% in 2022 to ~10% in 2024 — far behind China (48%) and Europe (20-90%). Tesla still dominates BEVs, but the cumulative US EV fleet of ~6.3 million is a fraction of China's. Federal IRA incentives, state mandates (CA), and Tesla/Ford/GM/Hyundai-Kia competition shape the trajectory.

stories.headline: Germany's EV share went backwards in 2024
stories.body: Germany hit 31% EV sales share in 2022, then fell to 24% in 2023 and 20% in 2024 after the abrupt end of the federal EV purchase subsidy in December 2023. A vivid case study in how policy reversals can stall an EV transition mid-flight.

caveats: OWID 'electric car sales share' combines BEV + PHEV. Use the separate 'bev-share-new-ev' dataset to split powertrains. Norway 2024: 92% combined = ~88% BEV + ~3% PHEV per IEA.

All headline figures sourced from IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 (published May 2025, covering through 2024). The corrigendum (July 2025) revises some minor numbers — check IEA corrigendum PDF before final publication.

Values shown are OWID-rounded; raw IEA CSV (via IEA login) carries more precision. Some OWID values appear as integers (e.g., World=21, US=10) — these are rounded from finer IEA values (~20.x, ~10.x).

Cross-check sources (BloombergNEF, EV-Volumes, Rho Motion) sometimes report slightly different shares because of methodology (e.g., calendar vs registration date, light commercial included or not). Reported global 2024 share ranges roughly 17-22% across providers.
IEA CSV download requires a free IEA account login — not directly fetchable by an unauthenticated pipeline. OWID CSVs are the practical bake-in source, and they cite IEA as upstream.
EV stock numbers represent cumulative cars on the road, not annual sales. Stock includes both BEV and PHEV.

audit.summary: All 14 numeric claims align with the IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 figures as republished by Our World in Data. The only nit: Norway BEV-only share is more accurately 89% (88.9% per OFV/industry data) than 88%; if the figure is being pulled from a different IEA table, the claim should be 89%. The bev-share-new-ev OWID dataset is a different metric (BEV as % of EVs, 96.9% for Norway in 2024), so the source attribution for the Norway BEV claim should be the IEA 'Trends in electric car markets' page or OFV rather than that OWID grapher.

research.summary: China is the undisputed center of the global EV transition: in 2024 it produced roughly 70% of the world's NEV sales, with CPCA reporting 10.98 million passenger NEVs sold at retail (47.6% of all passenger car retail) and CAAM reporting 12.87 million NEVs across all segments (40.9% share). Its NEV share rocketed from ~4-5% in 2018 to nearly half the market in 2024, driven by the MIIT dual-credit mandate (a CAFC + NEV credit-trading system enacted 2017), ultra-cheap models like the Wuling Hongguang Mini EV (~$4,000) and BYD Seagull (~$9,700), and a brutal price war ignited by Tesla in late 2022 and escalated by BYD through 2024. BYD overtook Tesla in quarterly BEV deliveries for the first time in Q4 2023 (526,409 vs 484,507), and Chinese EV exports surpassed 1.25 million units in 2024.

stories.headline: Q4 2023: BYD dethrones Tesla as the world's top BEV seller for the first time
stories.body: For one quarter, the global EV order flipped. In the last three months of 2023, BYD delivered 526,409 pure battery-electric passenger cars worldwide, edging Tesla's 484,507 deliveries by 8.6%. Tesla still won the full year (1.81 million vs BYD's 1.57 million BEVs), but the Q4 milestone was symbolic: a Shenzhen-based company that started as a battery maker had, for one quarter, outsold the Silicon Valley company that defined the modern EV. BYD never looked back at the broader NEV crown — counting plug-in hybrids, it sold 3.02 million vehicles in 2023 and 4.26 million in 2024, dwarfing Tesla in total electrified volume.

stories.headline: The 2023-2024 price war: Tesla lit the fuse, BYD set the market on fire
stories.body: China's EV price war began in late 2022 when Tesla cut Model 3 and Model Y prices in China, then slashed again in January 2023. BYD answered ferociously: across 2023, Reuters calculated BYD cut prices on 13 models accounting for 93% of its China sales by an average of 17%. In February 2024, BYD escalated, lowering the Qin Plus EV start price by 15% and the Qin Plus DM-i hybrid by 20%, and trimming the Seagull — already China's best-selling EV — by another 5%. The carnage forced dozens of legacy brands to discount or exit; even Tesla cut Model 3 and Model Y prices repeatedly through 2024 to keep up. Margins collapsed across the industry while consumers got a $10,000 Seagull with 305 km of range.

stories.headline: The $4,000 EV: Wuling Hongguang Mini and the BYD Seagull
stories.body: Two cars define how China made EVs affordable. The Wuling Hongguang Mini EV — a joint venture between SAIC, GM and Wuling — launched in 2020 at 28,800 yuan (about $4,000), a city runabout with 120 km range that briefly outsold the Tesla Model 3 in China. The BYD Seagull, launched April 2023, took the formula upmarket: 305 km of real range, modern safety features, and a starting price of 69,800 yuan (~$9,700) after a March 2024 cut. By 2024 the Seagull was China's best-selling EV. For context, the average new car in the US sells for roughly $48,000 — BYD's Seagull is more than 50% cheaper than any new or used car on the average US dealer lot.

stories.headline: MIIT's dual-credit policy: the regulatory engine behind China's NEV boom
stories.body: In 2017, China's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) finalized the 'Parallel Management Measures' — universally called the dual-credit policy — that took effect in 2018. Automakers earn (or owe) two kinds of credits: Corporate Average Fuel Consumption (CAFC) credits for fleet efficiency, and NEV credits for producing electric and plug-in hybrid vehicles. Required NEV credit ratios were set at 10% in 2019, 12% in 2020, then 14%/16%/18% for 2021-2023. Deficit automakers must buy credits from NEV-heavy peers like BYD or Tesla — turning every legacy carmaker into a forced buyer of EV production. A July 2023 update cut credits per standard NEV by ~40%, signaling the policy was succeeding fast enough that the subsidy needed tightening.

stories.headline: China becomes the world's largest auto exporter — driven by EVs
stories.body: In 2023 China overtook Japan as the world's #1 vehicle exporter, and in 2024 the lead widened: total Chinese auto exports hit 5.86 million units, of which 1.25 million were EVs. BYD alone exported more than 400,000 vehicles in 2024 — still under 10% of its 4.26 million total deliveries — and projects 800,000 to 1 million exports in 2025. The surge prompted defensive tariffs: the EU imposed countervailing duties up to 35% on Chinese EVs in October 2024, and the US raised its tariff on Chinese EVs to 100%. Even so, Chinese brands surged in Mexico, Southeast Asia, and Brazil.

caveats: CPCA and CAAM report different NEV totals for the same year: CPCA covers passenger car RETAIL only (~10.98M in 2024, 47.6% share), while CAAM covers ALL vehicle segments including commercial vehicles, exports, and uses wholesale (~12.87M in 2024, 40.9% share). Always state which source you cite.

'NEV' in Chinese policy = BEV + PHEV + Fuel Cell. PHEV explicitly includes extended-range EVs (EREVs like Li Auto / AITO). Western 'EV share' often means BEV-only, which is roughly 60-65% of China's NEV total — do not conflate.

BYD's Q4 2023 BEV win over Tesla was for one quarter only; Tesla retained the full-year 2023 BEV crown (1.81M vs 1.57M BYD BEVs). BYD's NEV (BEV+PHEV) total of 3.02M in 2023 was the higher overall number widely reported as 'BYD overtakes Tesla'.
2020 share figure: sources cite 5.4% (IEA) to 6.3% — likely difference between full-year and Q4-only, and between passenger-only and total market.
Wuling Hongguang Mini EV starting price of 28,800 yuan refers to the base 120 km range trim launched in 2020; higher-range Macaron and GameBoy trims are 40,000-90,000 yuan.
BYD Seagull's 69,800 yuan / $9,700 price is the post-March-2024 Honor Edition starting MSRP. Earlier 2023 launch price was 73,800 yuan; later 2025 cuts pushed entry under $8,000.
Currency conversions use approximate 2024 rates (~7.2 yuan/USD); USD figures will drift with FX.

audit.summary: Most China NEV claims are confirmed against CAAM/CPCA primary-sourced reporting. Two notable issues: (1) China 2024 EV/NEV exports — the claimed 1.25M is slightly low; CAAM's official figure is 1.284M units (the 1.25M figure traces to IEA's "EV exports" definition, not CAAM's NEV exports — these should not be conflated). (2) The 2024 global share of 70% applies specifically to Jan–Nov 2024 per CPCA's Cui Dongshu, not full-year. Other historical share figures (2018 4.5%, 2020 5.4%, 2021 15.7%, 2022 25.6%, 2023 31.6%) check out against CAAM. BYD Q4 2023 526,409 BEV figure confirmed.

research.summary: The dominant electric-mobility story outside the West is small, cheap, and Asian: 2- and 3-wheelers are the most electrified road segment in the world, with ~9% of the global fleet already electric and ~15% of 2024 sales — and roughly 80% of that activity sits in China, India, and Southeast Asia. India alone now electrifies 42% of its three-wheelers (e-rickshaws crossed 700k units in just Jan-Nov 2025), Nepal hit a stunning 73% EV share of imported passenger cars on the back of cheap hydropower, Vietnam's VinFast sold 406k e-scooters domestically in 2025, and Chinese brands like BYD took 38.5% of Thailand's EV market. In Africa, battery-swap startups Ampersand (Rwanda), Spiro (six countries, 80k bikes), and Roam (Kenya) are electrifying the boda-boda and moto-taxi economy because petrol fuel is the single biggest cost in a driver's day — making EVs cheaper, not greener, the headline pitch.

stories.headline: The world's largest electric vehicle isn't a Tesla — it's the e-rickshaw
stories.body: India quietly runs the largest electric vehicle fleet on Earth that nobody talks about: the e-rickshaw. In FY2025, 42% of all three-wheelers sold in India were electric, and through November 2025 alone the country registered over 709,000 new electric 3-wheelers — most of them simple, lead-acid-battery passenger rickshaws built by small workshops and sold for under $2,000. Uttar Pradesh accounts for nearly 38% of national sales. The FAME-II subsidy (up to 40% off ex-factory price during its peak) accelerated the boom, but the deeper driver is economics: a driver earning $5-10 a day cannot afford petrol, and an e-rickshaw pays for itself in months. India and China together account for over 90% of all 3-wheeler sales on the planet.
stories.source_name: IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 + Autocar Professional

stories.headline: Nepal beats Norway: 73% of new cars are electric
stories.body: Nepal — a landlocked country with a GDP per capita under $1,500 — now imports more electric cars than gasoline ones. In fiscal year 2024/25, EVs made up 73% of all four-wheeled passenger vehicle imports, one of the highest shares on Earth (rivaling Norway). The reason is structural, not aspirational: over 90% of Nepal's electricity comes from domestic hydropower, while every drop of petrol must be trucked in from India. Kathmandu lawmakers slashed import duties on EVs to a fraction of what gasoline cars pay. The 2025 oil-supply crisis at Indian border crossings paid the bet off in real time, as EV drivers cruised past gas-station queues.

stories.headline: VinFast turned Vietnam into a domestic EV nation overnight
stories.body: In 2025, Vietnamese conglomerate VinFast delivered 175,099 electric cars and 406,453 electric scooters inside Vietnam — a 473% year-over-year jump for scooters and enough to make VinFast the best-selling automotive brand in the country, period. The Evo scooter line alone sold more than 250,000 units, leapfrogging Honda in a market that runs on two wheels. Vietnam now has a homegrown EV champion of a kind no other developing country can claim, built by leveraging billionaire Pham Nhat Vuong's Vingroup balance sheet to subsidize a national pivot.

stories.headline: In Kigali, going electric is a pay raise
stories.body: Rwanda's roughly 4,000 Ampersand electric motorcycles aren't sold as a climate product — they're sold as a 30-50% pay raise for moto-taxi drivers, because batteries swapped in under a minute cost less than petrol. As of May 2025 Ampersand had captured 13% of Kigali's motorcycle-taxi market and was processing more than 20,000 battery swaps per day across East Africa. Investors including British International Investment, TotalEnergies, and Acumen have funded a path to 13,000 bikes by early 2026 and an aspirational 600,000 by 2030. Rwanda has set a national target of 20% EV adoption by 2030.

stories.headline: Africa's electric-moto boom runs on swap stations, not chargers
stories.body: Battery-swap startup Spiro now operates ~80,000 electric motorcycles and 2,500 swap stations across Rwanda, Uganda, Kenya, Benin, Togo, and Cameroon — making it Africa's largest electric-mobility company after raising $100M in October 2025. Kenya's Roam, designing bikes alongside boda-boda riders, sells the Roam Air for about $1,500 with two swappable batteries and 180 km of range. In the first eight months of 2025, electric motorcycles hit 10% of all new motorcycle registrations in Kenya. The model that works in Africa isn't Western-style overnight charging — it's bodega-style swap kiosks every few blocks that let drivers stay earning.

stories.headline: Southeast Asia became China's EV export beachhead
stories.body: Thailand's 2024 EV market reached 10% of new car sales — and 85% of those EVs were Chinese-brand. BYD alone took 38.5% of Thailand's electric car market with 70,137 units sold, helped by Thai EV-incentive packages that demand local assembly in exchange for tax breaks. In Indonesia, EV sales jumped 2.5x to 43,000 units in 2024 (about 5% of new vehicles); BYD entered late and immediately overtook incumbent Wuling. For Chinese automakers facing tariff walls in Europe and the US, ASEAN became the relief valve — and for ASEAN consumers, it meant affordable EVs years ahead of when domestic supply could deliver them.

India 'electric 3-wheeler share = 42%' is for FY2025 from IMARC summarizing VAHAN; the IEA pegs the global e-3W sales share at ~25% for 2024 — the difference is that India is at the high end and pulls the global average up.
Nepal's 73% EV-of-imports figure is for PASSENGER 4-wheelers and is import-share, not stock share. The on-road fleet is still majority ICE because EV imports only surged after FY2022/23.

VinFast figures are self-reported by the manufacturer and have been disputed by analysts who note heavy intra-group sales to Vingroup-owned taxi operator Green & Smart Mobility (GSM). True retail share is somewhat lower.
Ampersand and Spiro fleet counts are company-reported and grow monthly; cited values are as of mid-to-late 2025 / early 2026 and will be stale quickly.
Indonesia 2024 EV sales of 43,000 / 5% share is BEV only; some sources include hybrids and report higher 'electrified' shares — do not conflate.
Thailand's '85% Chinese-brand share of EV sales' includes BYD, MG (SAIC), Neta, GWM, Changan; the residual 15% is mostly Tesla, BMW, Mercedes-Benz, and locally assembled Japanese plug-ins.
Multiple cited URLs carry 2026 dates because today is 2026-06-16 (per the environment); these are real, published pieces, not forecasts.

Nepal hydropower share of generation is closer to ~98-99% (capacity ~95%), not 90%. The IEA "9% stock" figure is correctly phrased as "more than 9%". VinFast, Thailand, India 2W, Nepal EV import share, and Jan-Nov 2025 e3W numbers all check out.

research.domain: Lithium-ion battery price history (BloombergNEF learning curve)
research.summary: BloombergNEF's annual Battery Price Survey shows volume-weighted-average lithium-ion battery PACK prices fell from roughly $1,183/kWh in 2010 to $115/kWh in 2024 — a >90% decline in real terms over 14 years and the canonical "learning curve" for clean tech. The trajectory was a near-monotonic decline through 2021 ($132/kWh), interrupted by a single rise to $151/kWh in 2022 driven by lithium and nickel spikes, then a sharp resumption: $139/kWh in 2023 and $115/kWh in 2024 (the largest yearly drop since 2017). The 2024 plunge was driven by cell-manufacturing overcapacity (3.1 TWh capacity vs ~1.2 TWh demand), the shift from NMC to cheaper LFP chemistry, and collapsing lithium prices. BNEF's 2024 survey also reported BEV-only packs at $97/kWh — crossing the symbolic $100/kWh "EV-ICE parity" threshold for the first time. (BNEF's December 2025 survey, published after this site's vintage, shows a further drop to $108/kWh; included as a caveat.)

datasets.name: Hitting the EV Inflection Point (BNEF deck, May 2021)

caveats: NOMINAL vs REAL: BNEF reports each year's figure in REAL terms in that year's currency (e.g., 2024 survey is real 2024$; 2021 survey is real 2021$). Stitching the press releases gives a series that mixes base years. For a clean inflation-adjusted line, rebase everything to a single year using US CPI. The May 2021 BNEF deck provides 2010-2020 already harmonized to real 2020$.

2010 starting value disagrees across sources: BNEF cites '$1,100/kWh' (nominal), '$1,183/kWh' (real 2020$), and 'above $1,200/kWh' (real 2021$) in different press releases. All three describe the same underlying 2010 survey result; the spread is the rebasing year.

PACK vs CELL: This dataset is PACK prices (cells + casing + cooling + BMS). BNEF cell prices are roughly 78% of pack price as of 2023 (cell-to-pack ratio has risen from ~70% in the 2010s). Do NOT mix BNEF pack with the OWID/Ziegler-Trancik CELL series shown on Our World in Data.

The 2022 RISE ($132 -> $151/kWh) was the first ever YoY increase in the 12-year BNEF survey, driven by lithium carbonate spiking from ~$10k/t to >$70k/t and nickel/cobalt volatility post-Ukraine invasion. By 2023 prices resumed declining.

$100/kWh PARITY: BNEF long forecast pack prices would hit $100/kWh around 2024 — the threshold widely cited as enabling EV-ICE upfront-cost parity. The fleet-average $115/kWh passed it for the broader market in 2024; the BEV-only segment crossed at $97/kWh in 2024 and $99/kWh in 2025.

VINTAGE NOTE: The site brief specifies 2024 as the headline year ($115/kWh). BNEF's December 2025 survey (post-vintage) reports a further drop to $108/kWh; included as one row for completeness but not the headline.

BNEF's full surveys are PAID products. All figures cited here come from BNEF's free press release summaries on about.bnef.com — the granular breakdowns (per-segment, per-region, per-chemistry beyond headline numbers) require a BNEF subscription.

audit.summary: BNEF pack-price series 2010-2023 is essentially correct against BNEF's own press releases, but the dataset is OUTDATED — BNEF has since published 2024 ($115/kWh) and 2025 ($108/kWh) figures that the claimed list omits. Also: the OWID dataset URL is dead (404), and there is a subtle real-dollar-basis caveat — BNEF restates the entire historical series each year in that year's real dollars, so quoting 2019 in "real 2020$" vs "real 2019$" vs "real 2023$" gives slightly different numbers (e.g., 2019 is variously $157, $156). For a clean public site, pick one vintage of the BNEF series (the most recent, real 2025$) and use it end-to-end, rather than splicing each year from the survey published that year.

research.domain: EV purchase cost over time and affordability
research.summary: US EV average transaction prices (ATPs) have fallen sharply from a June 2022 peak of $66,390 to roughly $56k-$59k in 2024-2025, while the overall new-vehicle ATP has climbed to record highs above $50,000 - narrowing the EV-to-ICE price gap from ~$17k in 2022 to ~$9k in 2025 (Cox Automotive / Kelley Blue Book). Globally, BEV affordability is highly regional: China has already reached price parity with ICE in the SUV segment, while Europe and the US still carry a premium. The world's cheapest EVs cluster in China (Wuling Hongguang Mini EV from ~$4,950, BYD Seagull from ~$8,000) and India (Tata Tiago EV from ~$8,400); European 'budget' EVs (Dacia Spring, Citroen e-C3, BYD Dolphin Surf) cost 2-3x their Chinese counterparts due to tariffs, regulation, and content upgrades.

data_points.metric: EV ATP premium over industry average

stories.headline: The EV-to-ICE price gap is closing - but slowly, and unevenly
stories.body: In June 2022 the average new EV cost $66,390 in the US - roughly $17,000 more than the average new car. By late 2025 the EV ATP had fallen to around $58,000-$59,000 while the overall industry average climbed past $50,000 for the first time, narrowing the gap to about $9,000. The convergence stalled in late 2025: after IRA-backed EV incentives expired in September, lower-cost EV volume collapsed and the mix shifted toward luxury, pushing the gap back up. Sticker prices still understate true affordability for buyers who qualified for the (now-ended) $7,500 federal tax credit.

stories.headline: Price parity, already here in the SUV segment
stories.body: China crossed a quiet milestone in 2024: more than half of battery-electric SUV sales were priced below the average ICE SUV, per the IEA. SUVs are China's largest segment, about half of all car sales. Aggressive competition among BYD, Geely, Wuling and dozens of newcomers - plus the world's cheapest battery packs - has driven sticker prices to the point that an EV is often the value choice, not the premium one.

stories.headline: The $5,000 EV that outsold the Model 3 in China
stories.body: Built by the SAIC-GM-Wuling joint venture, the Hongguang Mini EV starts at about 35,000 yuan (~$4,950) for a 9.3 kWh, 75-mile-range city car. It has sold more than 1.9 million units cumulatively since 2020 and remains, by a wide margin, the cheapest mass-market EV on Earth. A new 2026 five-door version starts at ~$6,500.

stories.headline: An $8,000 car at home, a $26,000 car in Europe
stories.body: The BYD Seagull starts under $8,000 in China after a 2025 price cut. Exported to Europe as the 'Dolphin Surf' with a longer body, upgraded battery, and crash-structure changes, the same car starts at 22,990 EUR (~$26,000) - nearly 3x the China price. EU tariffs on Chinese EVs plus content upgrades explain most of the gap. The Seagull crossed 1 million cumulative global sales in mid-2025.

stories.headline: Tata's Tiago EV: India's $8,400 electric hatchback
stories.body: Tata Motors launched the Tiago EV at ~$10,400 in 2022 and trimmed entry pricing to Rs 6.99 lakh (~$8,400) by 2026. With 19.2-24 kWh battery options, it's India's most affordable mass-market EV and the volume leader alongside the Tata Tigor EV and MG Comet. India's price points are dramatically below the West but still far above China's micro-EVs.

stories.headline: Europe's 'cheap' EV is still 17-25k euros
stories.body: Europe's most affordable EVs - the Dacia Spring (from 16,990 EUR in France), Citroen e-C3 (from 19,990 EUR), and BYD Dolphin Surf (from 22,990 EUR) - all sit at price points that would be considered premium in China. Stricter crash and emissions homologation, EU tariffs on Chinese imports, higher labor costs, and a market that won't accept sub-100-mile city cars in volume all push the European 'budget' floor up. The UK's enhanced Dacia Spring drops to 12,240 GBP after a 3,750 GBP manufacturer subsidy.

caveats: STICKER vs OUT-THE-DOOR: US 'ATP' figures are transaction prices INCLUDING dealer/OEM incentives but BEFORE federal/state tax credits. Through Sept 30, 2025 a qualifying buyer could subtract up to $7,500 (new) or $4,000 (used) from the effective price; the federal EV tax credit was repealed for vehicles purchased after Sept 30, 2025, which Cox cites as the reason EV ATP rose and mix shifted to luxury in Oct-Dec 2025.
BEV vs BEV+PHEV: Cox/KBB's 'EV ATP' refers to BEVs only. IEA affordability data typically reports BEV prices separately from PHEV.
Currency conversions for cheapest-EV prices use mid-2025 FX rates. The BYD Seagull 'under $8,000' figure is the post-April-2025 China promotional price; standard MSRP was ~$8,900-$9,700.

China vs export pricing for the same model can differ 2-3x (Seagull $8k -> Dolphin Surf $26k) due to EU tariffs on Chinese EVs (provisional countervailing duties of 17-38% applied in late 2024), homologation upgrades (crash structure, ADAS), longer body, and dealer margins. 'Cheapest EV' globally requires noting domestic-market vs export price.

ICE comparison: KBB does not break out an ICE-only ATP; the 'industry average' includes ICE, hybrid, PHEV, and BEV. Implied ICE-only ATP is slightly lower than the industry average (since BEV ATP is higher), typically by ~$1-2k.

Price parity claims are highly segment- and region-specific. IEA's 2024 finding of BEV-ICE parity applies to China SUVs specifically; small cars in China reached parity earlier, while US large pickups and luxury sedans have not.

research.domain: renewable / low-carbon share of electricity by country (powers EV Atlas "how clean is your EV really?")
research.summary: The two authoritative open datasets are Ember's Yearly Electricity Data (CC BY 4.0, 215 countries, updated twice a month, with 2025 data for 91 countries) and Our World in Data's Electricity Mix graphers, which republish Ember + EI Statistical Review under CC BY. Both provide a clean direct-CSV download. Globally, low-carbon sources (renewables + nuclear) supplied ~40.9% of electricity in 2024 per Ember's Global Electricity Review 2025. Country leaders are Iceland, Paraguay, Norway, Switzerland and Sweden (all ~95-100%, mostly hydro), France (~90%+ nuclear-dominated), while coal-heavy Poland (~33%) and gas/coal-heavy Japan (~35%) and South Korea (~38%) trail. The US sits around 41-43%. For US per-state cleanness we will reuse Grid Atlas's existing 2001-2025 series rather than re-source.

Includes pre-aggregated 'Share of generation (%)' rows for Renewables, Clean (= low-carbon = renewables + nuclear), Fossil.

data_points.source_name: Our World in Data - Low-carbon electricity by country

data_points.metric: Low-carbon electricity share (renewables only, post nuclear phase-out)

caveats: DEFINITION: 'Low-carbon' = renewables (hydro + solar + wind + bioenergy + geothermal + wave/tidal) PLUS nuclear. 'Renewables' EXCLUDES nuclear. The gap matters enormously: France is ~95% low-carbon but only ~25-30% renewable; Germany (post nuclear phase-out, 2024) is ~59% low-carbon and that is ~all renewables. Pick one definition consistently and label it on every chart.

Sources sometimes disagree by 1-3 percentage points because of (a) different vintages (2023 vs 2024 vs 2025 partials), (b) whether 'other' / pumped-storage / waste-heat are bucketed as renewable, and (c) whether bunkers and own-use are netted out. OWID/Ember CSV is the canonical source; lowcarbonpower.org tracks Ember but updates more aggressively to 2025 partial data and is used here only where 2024 was unavailable in the OWID excerpt.
Norway and Iceland both round to ~100% in popular framings, but Ember's exact 2024 figures are ~98-99%, not 100%. A small fraction of gas/diesel backup remains.

For Germany 2024, all low-carbon generation was renewable - the last 3 nuclear reactors closed in April 2023. Pre-2023 series include nuclear; the time-series will show a step-down in nuclear share that needs annotating.
Brazil's hydro share fluctuates year-to-year with drought (e.g. 2021 reservoir lows pushed gas higher); use 5-year smoothing if a single-year snapshot would mislead.
Ember marks 2025 data as 'preliminary' for most countries until the Q4 update; for stable comparisons use 2024 as the headline year and show 2025 with a 'preliminary' badge.
US PER-STATE clean-electricity series will be REUSED from Grid Atlas (EIA-based 2001-2025) - do not re-source from Ember (Ember is country-level only for the US, not state-level).

Treat all fetched values as needing a final verification pass against the Ember Yearly Electricity Data CSV at bake time; the headline numbers above are accurate to ~1 pp but the pipeline should snap to the official CSV.

audit.summary: Most claimed 2024 low-carbon electricity shares match the OWID/Ember CSV to two decimal places (France 94.90, Brazil 89.45, Denmark 89.19, Canada 77.69, Iceland 100). Global 40.9% and 41% (OWID rounding) confirmed against the Ember Global Electricity Review 2025. UK at 59% is on the high edge of what sources show (Carbon Brief reports 58% for 2024; Ember's UK-specific writeup says ~58% renewables+nuclear); 59% is within rounding tolerance of OWID/Ember data so flagged as imprecise rather than wrong. New Zealand 88% is consistent with Ember-based lowcarbonpower estimate (renewables 85.5% + small geothermal share counted as low-carbon ≈88%). All dataset URLs resolve and host what's claimed, except that the Global Electricity Review 2026 has now been published (with 2025 data), so the 2026 supporting-materials URL is live and confirmed.

research.summary: A verified timeline of electric vehicle history from the 1830s Scottish carriages to 2025 mass adoption. EVs were once the dominant form of US automobile around 1900 (38% electric vs 40% steam vs 22% gasoline) before cheap oil, the Model T, and the electric self-starter wiped them out for nearly a century. The modern revival traces through GM's killed EV1, Toyota's Prius, Tesla's Roadster/Model S/Model 3, and a 2020s inflection where global EV sales hit ~14M in 2023 and >20% of new cars sold in 2024.

events.title: Robert Anderson's Electric Carriage
events.body: Scottish inventor Robert Anderson builds a crude electric carriage powered by non-rechargeable primary cells. It is the first known electric vehicle, but with no way to recharge the batteries it remains a curiosity, not transportation.

events.title: Thomas Davenport Builds the First American DC Motor
events.body: Vermont blacksmith Thomas Davenport and his wife Emily construct the first American direct-current electric motor. A year later he uses it to drive a small electric car around a circular track — the first electric railway. He patents the design in 1837.

events.title: Robert Davidson's 'Galvani' Electric Locomotive
events.body: Scotsman Robert Davidson runs a battery-powered locomotive named Galvani on the Edinburgh-Glasgow line. The 7-ton machine reaches 4 mph but burns through expensive single-use zinc-acid batteries. Steam workers — fearing for their jobs — destroy it.

events.title: Gaston Planté Invents the Rechargeable Battery
events.body: French physicist Gaston Planté builds the first practical rechargeable battery — the lead-acid cell — using two lead sheets separated by rubber, immersed in sulfuric acid. It's the breakthrough that makes electric vehicles actually possible.

events.title: William Morrison's Electric Wagon in Iowa
events.body: Chemist William Morrison debuts a six-passenger electric wagon in a Des Moines parade. It tops out around 14 mph on 24 lead-acid cells and is widely considered the first successful electric vehicle in the United States.
events.source_name: US Department of Energy: History of the Electric Car

events.title: London's 'Hummingbird' Electric Cabs
events.body: Walter Bersey launches a fleet of 12 electric hackney cabs on London's streets — nicknamed 'Hummingbirds' for the sound they made. The fleet grows to 75 cabs, but heavy weight chews through tires and batteries; the service collapses by 1899.

events.title: New York City's First Electric Taxi Fleet
events.body: The Samuel's Electric Carriage and Wagon Company puts 12 electric hansom cabs on New York streets. Within a year the fleet grows to 62 cabs. The operation is absorbed into the Electric Vehicle Company, which would run roughly 100 NYC taxis.

events.title: EVs Rule the American Road
events.body: At the dawn of the auto age, electric cars are winning. Of 4,192 US automobiles counted in the 1900 Census, 38% are electric, 40% are steam, and just 22% are gasoline. Electric cars are quiet, clean, and require no hand-cranking — beloved especially by city drivers.
events.source_name: US Department of Energy: History of the Electric Car

events.title: Baker Motor Vehicle Company Founded
events.body: Baker Electric is founded in Cleveland, Ohio. By 1906 it is the largest electric car maker in the US, building 800 cars per year. Thomas Edison himself drives a Baker — calling it the perfect vehicle.

events.title: Detroit Electric Begins Production
events.body: The Anderson Electric Car Company starts building Detroit Electrics, which become the longest-lasting EV brand of the era — over 13,000 sold by 1939. With an 80-mile range, they are favorites of Clara Ford and Mrs. Thomas Edison.
events.source_name: Edison Tech Center: History of Electric Cars

events.title: Ford Launches the Model T
events.body: Henry Ford's Model T arrives at $850, plunging to $260 by the 1920s thanks to assembly-line mass production. Cheap, simple gasoline transport is suddenly within reach of working families — the first existential threat to the electric car.
events.source_name: US Department of Energy: History of the Electric Car

events.title: The Electric Self-Starter Kills the Crank
events.body: Cadillac introduces Charles Kettering's electric self-starter, ending the era of dangerous hand-cranking gasoline engines. The single biggest selling point of electric cars — easy starting — evaporates overnight.

events.title: Edison and Ford's Electric Car That Never Was
events.body: The New York Times confirms Henry Ford and Thomas Edison are collaborating on a cheap electric car using Edison's nickel-iron batteries. Ford reportedly loans Edison $1.2M for 100,000 batteries. But the partnership fizzles and the car never reaches market.

events.title: Cheap Texas Oil Buries the EV
events.body: With Texas crude flowing and gas stations spreading along new highways, gasoline cars get cheaper, faster, and longer-range. By the late 1920s electric vehicles have all but disappeared from American roads.
events.source_name: US Department of Energy: History of the Electric Car

events.title: The Henney Kilowatt — An EV Before Its Time
events.body: Built on a Renault Dauphine body, the Henney Kilowatt is America's first modern production electric car. Only 47 are built across 1959-60; most go to electric utilities. With a 40-mile range and 30 mph top speed, it can't beat the gas car. The project folds.

events.title: GM's Electrovair and Electrovan
events.body: GM unveils the Electrovair II — an electric Chevrolet Corvair — and the Electrovan, the world's first hydrogen fuel cell vehicle. Both are experimental. The Electrovan's platinum-laden fuel cells cost so much GM jokes they could 'buy a fleet of vans.'

events.title: OPEC Embargo Sends US Back to EVs
events.body: The Arab Oil Embargo quadruples oil prices and lines snake around US gas stations. Suddenly electric vehicle research gets 'apocalyptic urgency and essentially unlimited funding.' Every Detroit automaker reopens EV programs.

events.title: Sebring-Vanguard CitiCar Hits the Road
events.body: Built in Sebring, Florida, the boxy little CitiCar tops 30 mph with a 40-mile range. About 2,300 are sold, making Sebring-Vanguard briefly the sixth-largest US automaker by 1975 — and holding the US EV production record until the Tesla Model S.

events.title: Congress Passes the EV R&D Act Over Ford's Veto
events.body: Congress overrides President Ford's veto to enact the Electric and Hybrid Vehicle Research, Development, and Demonstration Act — a 5-year, $160M federal program to develop a practical EV and put 7,500 demonstration vehicles on the road.

events.title: GM Unveils the Impact at the LA Auto Show
events.body: GM Chairman Roger Smith unveils the Impact, a sleek electric concept developed with AeroVironment. Sensing momentum, California's Air Resources Board responds with a Zero-Emission Vehicle mandate: 2% of major automakers' fleet must be ZEV by 1998.

events.title: GM EV1 Deliveries Begin
events.body: GM begins delivering the EV1 — the first mass-produced electric car from a major automaker in nearly 90 years. Lease-only at $399-$549/month, available only in California and Arizona. Just over 1,100 are built before the program is killed.

events.title: Toyota Prius Launches in Japan
events.body: Toyota launches the first-generation Prius — the world's first mass-produced hybrid passenger car. It sells 3,500 units in its first month and wins Japan's Car of the Year for 1998. The Prius makes 'hybrid' a household word.

events.title: GM Kills the EV1 — and Crushes the Cars
events.body: GM cancels the EV1 program, refuses to renew leases, and recalls every car. Despite a $1.9M offer from drivers to buy them back, GM sends almost all EV1s to be crushed. The destruction becomes a rallying cry for EV advocates.
events.source_name: Wikipedia: Who Killed the Electric Car?

events.title: Tesla Motors Founded
events.body: Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning incorporate Tesla Motors in San Carlos, California — named after Nikola Tesla. They are inspired in part by GM's destruction of the EV1, betting that lithium-ion can finally make a fast, practical electric car.

events.title: 'Who Killed the Electric Car?' Hits Theaters
events.body: Chris Paine's documentary, narrated by Martin Sheen, tells the story of the GM EV1's creation and destruction. It indicts oil companies, automakers, CARB, and the federal government — and turns EVs into a cultural cause.
events.source_name: Wikipedia: Who Killed the Electric Car?

events.title: First Tesla Roadster Delivered
events.body: Tesla delivers Roadster #0001 to co-founder Ian Wright. With a Lotus chassis, a lithium-ion battery, and a 0-60 in under 4 seconds, the Roadster proves an electric car can be sexy and fast. About 2,450 are built through 2012.

events.title: Nissan Leaf and Chevy Volt Reach Customers
events.body: On the same December weekend, the first Nissan Leaf (all-electric) is delivered in California and the first Chevy Volts (plug-in hybrid) leave a Detroit plant. The mass-market EV era officially begins.

events.title: Tesla Model S Begins Customer Deliveries
events.body: Tesla delivers the first Model S sedans at its Fremont factory. With 200+ miles of range, OTA software updates, and a 17-inch touchscreen, it transforms the EV from compliance car to status symbol. Motor Trend names it 2013 Car of the Year.

events.title: Model 3 Unveiled — 400,000 Preorders
events.body: Tesla unveils the $35,000 Model 3 in Hawthorne, California. Within 24 hours, 115,000 people put down $1,000 deposits sight-unseen. Within two weeks reservations approach 400,000 — the biggest product launch in auto history.

events.title: Model 3 Production Begins
events.body: Tesla starts shipping the Model 3 in late 2017, kicking off what Elon Musk calls 'production hell.' By 2018 it's outselling every other EV in the world and becomes the bestselling premium sedan in the US.

events.title: Tesla Model Y Launches + Shanghai Surge
events.body: Tesla begins delivering the Model Y crossover in early 2020. Meanwhile its new Shanghai gigafactory ramps Model 3 production for the China market — China explodes from niche to the world's largest EV market.

events.title: Rivian R1T Beats Tesla to Electric Trucks
events.body: Startup Rivian delivers the first R1T — the first electric pickup truck in the US market — months before Ford. Eight months later, in May 2022, Ford begins delivering the F-150 Lightning, electrifying America's bestselling vehicle.

events.title: Biden Signs the Inflation Reduction Act
events.body: The IRA — the largest climate law in US history — restructures the federal EV tax credit. Credits of up to $7,500 now require North American final assembly, plus battery sourcing rules that reshape global supply chains.

events.title: Norway Hits 79% EV Market Share
events.body: In 2022 nearly 4 in 5 new cars sold in Norway are electric — 79.3% counting BEVs plus PHEVs (82.4% pure battery-electric over the year). Norway proves a developed economy can flip its new-car market to electric in a decade.

events.title: BYD Overtakes Tesla in Quarterly BEV Sales
events.body: For the first time, Chinese automaker BYD outsells Tesla in a single quarter — 595,413 battery-electric vehicles to Tesla's 484,507. Tesla still wins the full year (1.8M vs 1.57M), but the Chinese EV industry has clearly arrived.
events.stat: BYD 595k vs Tesla 485k BEVs in Q4

events.title: Global EV Sales Hit ~14 Million
events.body: Roughly 14 million electric cars (BEV+PHEV) are sold worldwide in 2023 — nearly 1 in 5 new cars sold globally. China accounts for about 60% of registrations. The world has crossed the EV tipping point.

events.title: Battery Prices Fall to $115/kWh
events.body: BloombergNEF reports lithium-ion battery pack prices fall 20% in 2024 to a global average of $115/kWh — the biggest one-year drop since 2017. In China, packs fall to $94/kWh. EV battery prices drop below $100/kWh for the first time.

events.title: EVs Cross 20% of Global New-Car Sales
events.body: Global electric car sales exceed 17 million in 2024, with EVs accounting for more than 20% of the worldwide new-car market for the first time. The internal combustion engine, after a 120-year reign, is now visibly losing ground.

caveats: 1900 US sales split (38% electric / 40% steam / 22% gasoline) is the figure published by US DOE, derived from the 1900 Census; some sources phrase it as 'a third of cars on the road' rather than as sales share — use the DOE framing.
Robert Anderson's electric carriage is typically dated 'c. 1832-1839'; no patent exists. Some sources cite 1832, others 1837 or 1839.
BloombergNEF's 2024 battery pack price was reported as $115/kWh (Dec 2024 survey); a later Dec 2025 BNEF survey reports $108/kWh for 2025 — use $115/kWh for 2024.
Tesla Roadster first delivery date: Tesla's own messaging cites Feb 2008 for VIN #0001 (Ian Wright); the first US customer (George Clooney) took delivery in June 2008.
Charles Kettering's self-starter was first installed in a 1912 Cadillac (some sources cite 1911 patent filing, 1915 patent grant).
Norway 2022 figure: BEV-only share was 82.4%; combined BEV+PHEV share was 79.3% if counting full-year and 87.8% by another counting method — sources differ slightly.

checks.correct_value: c. 1832–1839; exact year uncertain
checks.note: Sources vary; Anderson is widely credited with a crude electric carriage 'around 1832,' but historians often give the range 1832–1839 because no contemporary record pins the date precisely. Phrasing as exactly 1832 is the popular shorthand but is not firmly documented.

checks.claim: 1834: Thomas Davenport builds the first American DC motor
checks.correct_value: 1834 (motor built); first U.S. patent on an electric machine granted 1837 (Patent No. 132)
checks.note: Davenport, a Vermont blacksmith, built his DC motor in 1834. The patent itself came in 1837. If the timeline implies the patent was 1834, that would be wrong; the motor build date is correct.

checks.correct_value: 1842, tested on Edinburgh–Glasgow line in September 1842
checks.note: Confirmed by multiple sources including Wikipedia and Heritage Railway Magazine.

checks.claim: 1859: Gaston Planté invents the rechargeable battery
checks.correct_value: 1859 (lead-acid cell); demonstrated to French Academy of Sciences in 1860
checks.note: Planté invented the lead-acid rechargeable battery in 1859. Confirmed by Britannica, Wikipedia, National MagLab. Note: one search result erroneously said 1865 — that is wrong; 1859 is correct.

checks.claim: 1890: William Morrison's electric wagon in Iowa
checks.correct_value: 1890–1891 (Des Moines parade debut 1890); an earlier prototype existed in 1887
checks.note: The six-passenger wagon that became famous debuted on Des Moines streets in 1890 and was refined through 1891. Britannica gives '1890–91.' Pinning it to a single year (1890) is the common shorthand and defensible, but '1890–91' is more accurate.

checks.correct_value: Service began 19 August 1897 (Bersey Electric Cab)
checks.note: Bersey cabs were nicknamed 'hummingbirds' for their motor hum and bright yellow/black livery. Service began August 1897.

checks.claim: 1897: New York City's first electric taxi fleet
checks.correct_value: Electric Carriage and Wagon Company began service in 1897 (often cited as July 1897; some sources say service began earlier in spring 1897)
checks.note: Confirmed — first NYC motorized taxi fleet was electric, operated by Electric Carriage and Wagon Co. (Morris & Salom), starting 1897. Sources vary slightly on the exact month (some say March/April, most popular sources say July 1897).

checks.correct_value: 1899, Cleveland, Ohio (Walter C. Baker)
checks.note: Confirmed by multiple sources.

checks.correct_value: June 1907 — production by Anderson Carriage Company under the 'Detroit Electric' brand (125 cars in 1907). Company renamed Anderson Electric Car Co. in 1911, Detroit Electric Car Co. in 1920.
checks.note: The 'Detroit Electric' brand started production in 1907; the company name only formally became 'Detroit Electric' in 1920. The claim is correct for the brand/production debut.

checks.correct_value: October 1908 (first Model T produced Sept 27, 1908; sold from October 1908)
checks.note: Well-documented and uncontroversial.

checks.claim: 1912: The electric self-starter kills the crank
checks.correct_value: Kettering's electric starter first demonstrated Feb 1911; patented June 15, 1911; first offered as standard equipment on 1912 Cadillac Model 30
checks.note: The 1912 date refers to deployment on the production Cadillac, not invention. Invention/demo was 1911, patent 1911. If the timeline says '1912 invention,' that's wrong; if it says '1912 introduction on Cadillac,' that's correct.

checks.claim: 1914: Edison and Ford's electric car that never was
checks.correct_value: Collaboration publicly announced January 11, 1914 in NYT; project never came to fruition
checks.note: Date confirmed. Some sources note Ford and Edison had been working on the idea earlier (since ~1912), but the public announcement and main publicity push was 1914.

checks.correct_value: Around 1900, electric cars accounted for roughly 38% of US auto market vs ~22% gasoline and ~40% steam (per US DOE), but 'rule the road' is an overstatement — total auto numbers were tiny and horses still dominated transport
checks.note: Commonly cited stat: in 1900, ~28% of the ~4,200 American automobiles produced were electric. EVs were the leading auto type but absolute numbers were minuscule.
checks.source_name: US Dept of Energy — History of the Electric Car

checks.claim: 1920: Cheap Texas oil buries the EV
checks.correct_value: The EV decline was a 1910s–1930s process; Texas oil discoveries (Spindletop 1901) and cheap gasoline contributed across this period, not in a single 1920 event
checks.note: 1920 is a defensible symbolic anchor (EV sales had collapsed by then; last Detroit Electric sold 1939). But there is no specific 1920 event tying 'cheap Texas oil' to burying the EV. Major Texas discoveries: Spindletop 1901, East Texas field 1930. The framing is editorial shorthand, not a dated event.

audit.summary: Most timeline events are confirmed against primary/authoritative sources. A few need minor precision tweaks: (1) Anderson's carriage is best dated 1832–1839, not strictly 1832; (2) Davenport's motor was built 1834 but patented 1837 — fine if labeled as the build date; (3) Morrison's wagon is more accurately 1890–91, with a 1887 prototype predating it; (4) the electric self-starter was invented/patented 1911 and first deployed on the 1912 Cadillac — the headline '1912' is correct only as deployment, not invention; (5) '1900: EVs rule the American road' is editorially loose — EVs led the small auto market but horses still dominated transport; (6) '1920: Cheap Texas oil buries the EV' is a symbolic anchor, not a dated event — Spindletop was 1901 and the EV decline spanned decades. All other claims (Davidson 1842, Planté 1859, London Hummingbirds 1897, NYC electric taxis 1897, Baker 1899, Detroit Electric production 1907, Model T 1908, Edison-Ford 1914) check out against primary sources.

research.summary: Global electric car sales (BEV+PHEV) grew from ~7,500 vehicles in 2010 to 17.5 million in 2024, with the EV share of new car sales climbing from essentially 0% to ~21% over the same period. The global EV stock reached ~58 million at end-2024, about 4% of the total passenger car fleet. China dominates with over 11 million sales in 2024 (~65% of the global total and ~50% domestic share), Europe sold ~3.2 million (~20% share, flat YoY), the US ~1.6 million (~10% share, +10% YoY), and the rest of the world ~1.3 million (+nearly 40%). IEA projects ~40% global EV sales share by 2030 under the Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS).

caveats: All sales/stock figures are BEV+PHEV combined (plug-in EVs); IEA's headline definition. Around 70% of 2024 global sales were BEV, 30% PHEV.
China 2024 share figure (~48%) is IEA's 'almost half'; some sources cite 53% for 2025 YTD. Use 'almost half' or ~48% for 2024.
Europe 2024 sales ~3.2M is approximate — IEA reports the share remained ~20% and sales 'stagnated'. EV-Volumes/Rho Motion put Europe 2024 at ~3.0-3.2M.
Rest-of-World 2024 sales of 1.3M (+~40% YoY) reported by IEA; this closely matches global total minus China+Europe+US (17.5M − 11M − 3.2M − 1.6M ≈ 1.7M, small discrepancy from rounding).

2030 projection: IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 STEPS scenario projects ~40% global EV sales share and >43 million electric LDV sales annually. Announced Pledges Scenario (APS) projects ~50% share. BloombergNEF EVO 2024 economic-transition scenario projects ~45% share by 2030, somewhat higher than IEA STEPS.
Historical pre-2015 sales figures are small and revised periodically; IEA data is the canonical series. EV-Volumes and Rho Motion (industry trackers) generally align within ±5% on recent years.

audit.summary: Most year-by-year totals (2010-2022) align with the IEA Global EV Outlook series as republished by Our World in Data. Two issues: (1) the 2023 claim of 13.7M is slightly low — IEA GEO 2024 reports "nearly 14 million" (~13.95M), and GEO 2025 carries it at ~14.0M; (2) the series stops at 2023 even though the cited source (Global EV Outlook 2025) extends through 2024, when global sales exceeded 17 million — a critical missing data point. Dataset URLs are valid. The IEA report PDF URL resolves correctly.
